The GET Request That Never Ends: A Denial-of-Service Hiding in Plain Sight
Picture this: you’ve built an API. Every sensitive route sits behind auth middleware. Then someone sends a single GET request — no login, no token, nothing malicious-looking in any log you'd normally check — and it just... sits there. Not crashing. Not erroring. Just holding a connection open, silently, for as long as your infrastructure lets it.
Do that a few dozen times in parallel, and depending on how your stack is built, you can go from “fully operational” to “refusing every legitimate request” — without ever touching your authentication, without exploiting a single line of business logic, and without leaving behind the kind of error that makes anyone go looking.
This is a Denial of Service (DoS) — one of the oldest, most well-understood attack categories in web security, hiding inside a request that looks, on its face, completely harmless. This post is about why that’s possible, why it’s more possible than most teams assume, and — the part that should actually worry you — why the exact defenses you already believe are protecting you might not be wired up the way you think.
The one-line root cause
express.json() — the middleware powering body parsing in a huge share of Node.js APIs — will attempt to read a request body regardless of HTTP method. GET, HEAD, DELETE — none of these are supposed to carry a body per HTTP semantics, but Express doesn't check. If a client declares a Content-Length header and then simply doesn't send that many bytes, the parser blocks, waiting for data that's never coming.
curl -v --http1.1 -X GET 'http://localhost:3000/test' \
--header 'Content-Length: 20' \
--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \No body sent. The request hangs for the full duration of whatever timeout eventually intervenes — which, as we’ll get to, is doing a lot more heavy lifting than most people realize.
This holds identically across express@5.2.1 and its previous versions, and — because frameworks build on top of each other — it propagates straight into NestJS (@nestjs/platform-express@11.1.27), which delegates body parsing to the same underlying pipeline without adding its own guard.
“But my routes are authenticated” — here’s why that doesn’t help
This is the point that should actually change how you think about your own app, so it’s worth being precise.
In a typical Express app, middleware gets registered like this
app.use(express.json()); // registered globally, first
app.use('/api', authMiddleware); // registered after, per-routeThat ordering is extremely common — it’s what most tutorials show, and it’s the path of least resistance when scaffolding an app. But it means every request passes through the body parser before it ever reaches your auth check. An attacker doesn’t need a valid token, doesn’t need to know your route exists, doesn’t need anything — because the request never gets far enough into your middleware chain for auth to matter. It stalls in the parser, upstream of every protection you’ve built.
Now add a second, very ordinary piece of context, and this is one nearly every backend team runs into at some point: file import/export endpoints routinely need longer timeouts than the rest of the app. A CSV import, a bulk data upload, a large report export — these legitimately take longer than the default 20–60 seconds most reverse proxies allow out of the box. So a developer goes into the nginx/Apache/ALB config and bumps the timeout for that specific route, or sometimes for the whole proxy, from 60 seconds to 5 minutes, or higher. This is completely normal, necessary, unremarkable engineering — teams do this constantly, and there’s usually a specific, reasonable justification attached to the change.
The reasoning that usually accompanies it: “this route is auth-gated, so a longer timeout here is safe — only logged-in users can even reach it.”
That reasoning only holds if auth actually runs before the body parser in the middleware chain. If it doesn’t — and per the ordering shown above, it very often doesn’t — you’ve just taken the exact connection-hold window from the top of this post and deliberately made it five or ten times longer, specifically on the route an operator most carefully hardened, without gaining any of the protection “it’s authenticated” was supposed to provide. The timeout increase that was meant to accommodate one slow, legitimate, trusted upload instead becomes the window an anonymous attacker gets to hold a connection open, because the request never reaches far enough into the stack for “authenticated” to mean anything.
This is, in practice, the single most likely way this vulnerability turns from “theoretical resource pressure” into “a route with a genuinely dangerous configuration” — not through anything exotic, but through a completely ordinary, well-intentioned timeout change that nobody thought to re-audit against middleware order.What actually measured — not just theorized
What actually measured — not just theorized
Ran a concurrency test: 500 simultaneous forged requests, each declaring aContent-Length with no matching body.

+----------------------------------+----------+---------------------------+
| Metric | Baseline | After 500 forged requests |
+----------------------------------+----------+---------------------------+
| Open file descriptors | 22 | 522 |
| Memory (RSS) | 63MB | 75MB |
| Fresh, unrelated request latency | 8ms | 18ms |
+----------------------------------+----------+---------------------------+Node’s event loop stayed responsive throughout — this is not a CPU-exhaustion bug, and it’s important not to overstate it as one. What it is: a connection/file-descriptor/memory exhaustion pattern, in the same family as the 2009-era “slowloris” attack class, just reached through an additional, easier door than most teams threat-model for.
The safety net you’re actually relying on (and its real boundaries)
Here’s where this gets genuinely interesting, because the honest answer is more nuanced than “Express is broken” — most of the time, something does catch this. The question is what, and under what conditions.
Node.js itself has default protections: server.requestTimeout (5 minutes, default since v18.0.0) and server.headersTimeout (60 seconds, default since v18.14.0). Both exist specifically to bound this class of issue.
Reverse proxies, when properly configured, are even more effective — and we tested this directly rather than assuming it. A stock nginx instance with client_body_timeout set doesn't just eventually close the hung connection: with default request buffering enabled, it never even opens a connection to your backend at all for a request whose body never fully arrives. Your Express app can be sitting there, completely exposed at the code level, and never once see the malicious request — because nginx absorbed it entirely at the edge.
That’s genuinely reassuring. But it depends entirely on two things holding true: the proxy is configured with a sane timeout, and the version you’re running actually enforces the protections you’re assuming it has.
What to actually do with this

• Check your middleware order. If body-parsing middleware is registered before auth or rate-limiting, “this route is authenticated” is not protecting you from this pattern.

• Check your proxy version, not just its configuration. A perfectly configured client_body_timeout on nginx 1.20 doesn't save you from the smuggling-based bypass; the version itself matters as much as the directive.

• Don’t assume Node’s defaults are enough if you’re internet-facing. Five minutes is a long time to hold a connection open with nothing in front of you.

• If a route genuinely never accepts a body, don’t rely on the framework to know that. Add an explicit method check.
— — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
This is the first in a short series on this research. Next: a full breakdown of the cross-framework comparison and what “good defaults” actually look like in practice.
